args:
A 33-year-old man, without known co-morbidities, was brought into the emergency department with sudden onset of headache and severe central chest pain, radiating to the back. The patient arrived 3.5 hours after the onset of symptoms and was still in severe pain.
The blood pressure was 180/125 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute (bpm), temperature 36.1°C, oxygen-saturation 98% and the respiratory-rate was 28 per minute. An arterial blood gas (ABG) revealed significant lactate acidosis with pH 7.14, pO2 of 15.4 kPa, pCO2 of 4.5 kPa, bicarbonate of 12 mEq/L, base excess of −16 mmol/L and lactate value of 12 mmol/L. A 12 lead electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with slight ST-depressions in the leads V4–6. 
Bedside echocardiography revealed regional hypokinesia in the lateral area of the left ventricle, and an estimated left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 20–30%.
The patients’ blood samples demonstrated abnormal with a troponin T of 300 ng/L, white blood cell count of 39 × 109/L and normal c-reactive protein.
An immediate thoracic/abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan was performed, testing negative for aortic dissection or large pulmonary embolism. However, a 6 cm large tumor in the left adrenal gland was found in .
During the first hours of observation, the patient’s symptoms spontaneously resolved. His vitals normalized and a repeated ABG showed a rising pH (7.33) and decreasing lactate (5.6 mmol/L). 
Meanwhile, the patient’s previous history was explored in more detail. The evening before his admission, he had consumed multiple energy drinks, but denies the intake of alcohol or any other substances. 
Nine years earlier, he presented with similar symptoms at another hospital, likewise had spontaneously recovered and was quickly discharged without a firm diagnosis. Similarly, 4 years prior to this admission, the patient was acutely hospitalized with headache, back pain, nausea, elevated lactate, white blood cell count and blood pressure. That admission summary described that all symptoms remitted, his vitals as well as blood values normalized over a few hours and the patient was discharged after a day of observation, without finding a plausible explanation to his symptoms. 
Due to these findings and the suspicion of pheochromocytoma-induced cardiomyopathy, the patient was admitted. During the 7-day admission, the patient’s vitals remained normal and blood samples including troponin T slowly normalized. 
A repeated transthoracic echocardiography showed a LVEF of 45% in .
Plasma cortisol, renin, aldosterone and a 24-hour urine sample for steroid metabolites were within normal range. 3-Metthoxyadrenalin was elevated to 2,5 nmol/L (normal range: < 0,46 nmol/L) as well as 3-Metthoxynoradrenalin at 29,6 nmol/L (normal range < 1,09 nmol/L). 
During this observation, the patient reported no symptoms and was discharged for planned surgery in an outpatient setting. 
Due to the size of the tumor (6 cm in diameter) and hence a slightly increased risk of malignancy, open left-sided adrenalectomy was performed []. 
The patient was discharged on the fifth postoperative day after uncomplicated surgery and observation period. Follow-up showed normalization of 3-metthoxyadrenalin and 3-metthoxynoradrenalin, and the patient had not experienced any symptoms or complications. 
The left adrenal gland was found with a well-defined pheochromocytoma with a maximum diameter of 25 mm. The tumor morphology was with the growth of large nests and solid areas, high cellularity and areas with spindle cells as well as areas with profound nuclear pleomorphism and nuclear hyperchromasia. Thus, the Pheochromocytoma of Adrenal Gland Scales (PASS) score was 8 in []. It is not possible to predict the malign potential of a pheochromocytoma from the histopathology, but a PASS score > 4 indicates potential for aggressive clinical behavior. Thus, the patient will be followed with annually blood tests (3-metthoxyadrenalin, 3-metthoxynoradrenalin) lifelong. [].

args:
A 33-year-old man, without known co-morbidities, was brought into the emergency department with sudden onset of headache and severe central chest pain, radiating to the back. The patient arrived 3.5 hours after the onset of symptoms and was still in severe pain.
The blood pressure was 180/125 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats per minute (bpm), temperature 36.1°C, oxygen-saturation 98% and the respiratory-rate was 28 per minute. An arterial blood gas (ABG) revealed significant lactate acidosis with pH 7.14, pO2 of 15.4 kPa, pCO2 of 4.5 kPa, bicarbonate of 12 mEq/L, base excess of −16 mmol/L and lactate value of 12 mmol/L. A 12 lead electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with slight ST-depressions in the leads V4–6. 
Bedside echocardiography revealed regional hypokinesia in the lateral area of the left ventricle, and an estimated left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 20–30%.
The patients’ blood samples demonstrated abnormal with a troponin T of 300 ng/L, white blood cell count of 39 × 109/L and normal c-reactive protein.
An immediate thoracic/abdominal computed tomography (CT) scan was performed, testing negative for aortic dissection or large pulmonary embolism. However, a 6 cm large tumor in the left adrenal gland was found in .
During the first hours of observation, the patient’s symptoms spontaneously resolved. His vitals normalized and a repeated ABG showed a rising pH (7.33) and decreasing lactate (5.6 mmol/L). 
Meanwhile, the patient’s previous history was explored in more detail. The evening before his admission, he had consumed multiple energy drinks, but denies the intake of alcohol or any other substances. 
Nine years earlier, he presented with similar symptoms at another hospital, likewise had spontaneously recovered and was quickly discharged without a firm diagnosis. Similarly, 4 years prior to this admission, the patient was acutely hospitalized with headache, back pain, nausea, elevated lactate, white blood cell count and blood pressure. That admission summary described that all symptoms remitted, his vitals as well as blood values normalized over a few hours and the patient was discharged after a day of observation, without finding a plausible explanation to his symptoms. 
Due to these findings and the suspicion of pheochromocytoma-induced cardiomyopathy, the patient was admitted. During the 7-day admission, the patient’s vitals remained normal and blood samples including troponin T slowly normalized. 
A repeated transthoracic echocardiography showed a LVEF of 45% in .
Plasma cortisol, renin, aldosterone and a 24-hour urine sample for steroid metabolites were within normal range. 3-Metthoxyadrenalin was elevated to 2,5 nmol/L (normal range: < 0,46 nmol/L) as well as 3-Metthoxynoradrenalin at 29,6 nmol/L (normal range < 1,09 nmol/L). 
During this observation, the patient reported no symptoms and was discharged for planned surgery in an outpatient setting. 
Due to the size of the tumor (6 cm in diameter) and hence a slightly increased risk of malignancy, open left-sided adrenalectomy was performed []. 
The patient was discharged on the fifth postoperative day after uncomplicated surgery and observation period. Follow-up showed normalization of 3-metthoxyadrenalin and 3-metthoxynoradrenalin, and the patient had not experienced any symptoms or complications. 
The left adrenal gland was found with a well-defined pheochromocytoma with a maximum diameter of 25 mm. The tumor morphology was with the growth of large nests and solid areas, high cellularity and areas with spindle cells as well as areas with profound nuclear pleomorphism and nuclear hyperchromasia. Thus, the Pheochromocytoma of Adrenal Gland Scales (PASS) score was 8 in []. It is not possible to predict the malign potential of a pheochromocytoma from the histopathology, but a PASS score > 4 indicates potential for aggressive clinical behavior. Thus, the patient will be followed with annually blood tests (3-metthoxyadrenalin, 3-metthoxynoradrenalin) lifelong. [].

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 55)
history_suspicious: 0=slightly, 1=moderately, 2=highly suspicious
ecg_findings: 0=normal, 1=non-specific, 2=significant ST deviation
risk_factors: 0=none, 1=1-2 factors, 2=3+ factors
troponin: 0=normal, 1=1-3x normal, 2=>3x normal

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: history_suspected_acs, hypertension, abnormal_ecg, elevated_troponin
- Conditions ABSENT: age_45_above, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, smoking, family_history_acs, obesity
- Demographics: Age=33, Sex=male

args: A 38-year-old postpartum woman arrived to the ambulatory triage area late this afternoon because her left lower leg had become noticeably heavier and mildly painful over the preceding 24 hours. Six weeks ago she delivered her second child via uncomplicated scheduled cesarean section that required spinal anesthesia and an overnight stay; she was discharged on postoperative day 2 and has since resumed routine household activities without assistive devices. Yesterday morning, while standing at the kitchen counter for an extended period preparing bottles, she felt a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the calf that worsened when she bore weight and eased modestly with elevation. By evening she observed that her sock left an imprint on that side but not on the right. She denies antecedent trauma, ankle sprain, insect bite, recent intense exercise, or local skin infection. She also denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or constitutional complaints. There is no history of malignant disease, hematologic disorder, or prior venous clot, nor has she ever required anticoagulation. She reports no neurologic illness, limb weakness, or application of a plaster cast, and she has remained fully mobile caring for her newborn. She uses no estrogen-containing contraception and takes only prenatal vitamins and 600 mg ibuprofen as needed for incisional soreness. Both parents are alive and free of venous thromboembolic events; no siblings have clotting disorders. She has never smoked, drinks wine rarely, and denies illicit drug use. On presentation she appeared comfortable holding the infant carrier, spoke in complete sentences, and ambulated without assistance. Vital signs before any medications were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar that was nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated subtle fullness confined to the left calf; there was no erythema, hyperpigmentation, varicosity, or brawny skin change. A flexible tape placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38.0 cm on the left and 36.4 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.6 cm. Circumference at mid-thigh and around the ankle was symmetric. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the left foot produced a 1+ indentation that resolved within 15 seconds, whereas identical pressure on the right left no depression. Palpation along the course of the popliteal vein and posteromedial calf elicited focal discomfort described by the patient as “a deep bruise,” compared with no tenderness over the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from the neurovascular bundle produced only minimal diffuse soreness. There was no palpable subcutaneous cord and no crepitus. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain at the Achilles insertion. Skin temperature was marginally warmer over the symptomatic calf compared with the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2+ bilaterally, and capillary refill of all toes was under two seconds. Motor strength in ankle dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and great-toe extension was 5/5, and sensation to light touch and vibration was intact. Straight-leg raise and seated slump test were negative for radicular symptoms, making a spinal etiology improbable. There was no ipsilateral knee effusion, meniscal tenderness, or ligamentous laxity, and range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full. No inguinal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. Basic laboratory studies obtained prior to any antithrombotic therapy showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0 with activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. These values reveal no anemia, thrombocytopenia, or renal impairment that might confound subsequent management. Given the recent abdominal-pelvic surgical intervention within the past two months, the presence of unilateral pitting edema confined to the calf, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway, and the absence of a compelling musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or neurologic explanation, an acute thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system remains the leading consideration at this stage of evaluation.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 38-year-old postpartum woman (demographics: female, age 38). Evaluating for Wells' Criteria for DVT. Conditions: Active cancer? No, explicitly denied. Paralysis/paresis? No, motor strength intact, denies limb weakness. Recently bedridden >3 days? No, discharged POD2 and resumed routine activities. Localized tenderness along deep venous system? Yes, focal discomfort along popliteal vein and posteromedial calf. Entire leg swollen? No, swelling confined to left calf (1.6cm asymmetry). Calf swelling >3cm? Yes, 1.6cm difference (criteria requires >3cm, so absent). Pitting edema? Yes, 1+ indentation on left. Collateral superficial veins? No, no varicosity mentioned. Alternative diagnosis as likely? No, no musculoskeletal/dermatologic/neurologic explanation found. Conditions present: localized_tenderness, pitting_edema. Conditions absent: active_cancer, paralysis_paresis, recently_bedridden, entire_leg_swollen, calf_swelling_3cm, collateral_superficial_veins, alternative_diagnosis.

args: A 38-year-old postpartum woman arrived to the ambulatory triage area late this afternoon because her left lower leg had become noticeably heavier and mildly painful over the preceding 24 hours. Six weeks ago she delivered her second child via uncomplicated scheduled cesarean section that required spinal anesthesia and an overnight stay; she was discharged on postoperative day 2 and has since resumed routine household activities without assistive devices. Yesterday morning, while standing at the kitchen counter for an extended period preparing bottles, she felt a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the calf that worsened when she bore weight and eased modestly with elevation. By evening she observed that her sock left an imprint on that side but not on the right. She denies antecedent trauma, ankle sprain, insect bite, recent intense exercise, or local skin infection. She also denies fevers, chills, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, or constitutional complaints. There is no history of malignant disease, hematologic disorder, or prior venous clot, nor has she ever required anticoagulation. She reports no neurologic illness, limb weakness, or application of a plaster cast, and she has remained fully mobile caring for her newborn. She uses no estrogen-containing contraception and takes only prenatal vitamins and 600 mg ibuprofen as needed for incisional soreness. Both parents are alive and free of venous thromboembolic events; no siblings have clotting disorders. She has never smoked, drinks wine rarely, and denies illicit drug use. On presentation she appeared comfortable holding the infant carrier, spoke in complete sentences, and ambulated without assistance. Vital signs before any medications were temperature 37.0 °C, blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm with no murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation. Abdominal inspection showed a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar that was nontender with no palpable masses or organomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated subtle fullness confined to the left calf; there was no erythema, hyperpigmentation, varicosity, or brawny skin change. A flexible tape placed 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity measured 38.0 cm on the left and 36.4 cm on the right, a disparity of 1.6 cm. Circumference at mid-thigh and around the ankle was symmetric. Gentle thumb pressure over the anterior tibia and dorsum of the left foot produced a 1+ indentation that resolved within 15 seconds, whereas identical pressure on the right left no depression. Palpation along the course of the popliteal vein and posteromedial calf elicited focal discomfort described by the patient as “a deep bruise,” compared with no tenderness over the contralateral limb; squeezing the gastrocnemius muscle belly away from the neurovascular bundle produced only minimal diffuse soreness. There was no palpable subcutaneous cord and no crepitus. Passive dorsiflexion of the ankle did not reproduce pain at the Achilles insertion. Skin temperature was marginally warmer over the symptomatic calf compared with the right when assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand. Distal pulses—dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial—were 2+ bilaterally, and capillary refill of all toes was under two seconds. Motor strength in ankle dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, and great-toe extension was 5/5, and sensation to light touch and vibration was intact. Straight-leg raise and seated slump test were negative for radicular symptoms, making a spinal etiology improbable. There was no ipsilateral knee effusion, meniscal tenderness, or ligamentous laxity, and range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full. No inguinal lymphadenopathy was appreciated. Basic laboratory studies obtained prior to any antithrombotic therapy showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 248 × 10⁹/L, white blood cells 7.1 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, and an INR of 1.0 with activated partial thromboplastin time 29 seconds. These values reveal no anemia, thrombocytopenia, or renal impairment that might confound subsequent management. Given the recent abdominal-pelvic surgical intervention within the past two months, the presence of unilateral pitting edema confined to the calf, focal tenderness along the deep venous pathway, and the absence of a compelling musculoskeletal, dermatologic, or neurologic explanation, an acute thrombotic obstruction of the deep venous system remains the leading consideration at this stage of evaluation.

active_cancer: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months) (+1)
paralysis: Paralysis, paresis, or recent leg cast (+1)
bedridden: Bedridden > 3 days or major surgery within 12 weeks (+1)
localized_tenderness: Localized tenderness along deep venous system (+1)
leg_swelling: Entire leg swollen (+1)
calf_swelling_3cm: Calf swelling > 3cm compared to asymptomatic leg (+1)
pitting_edema: Pitting edema confined to symptomatic leg (+1)
collateral_veins: Collateral superficial veins (+1)
previous_dvt: Previously documented DVT (+1)
alternative_diagnosis: Alternative diagnosis at least as likely (-2)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: localized_tenderness, pitting_edema
- Conditions ABSENT: active_cancer, paralysis_paresis, recently_bedridden, entire_leg_swollen, calf_swelling_3cm, collateral_superficial_veins, alternative_diagnosis
- Demographics: Age=38, Sex=female

args: A 62-year-old female with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to the emergency department this morning complaining of a dull ache behind the right knee that began yesterday afternoon while she was attempting to ambulate from her bedroom to the bathroom. Nine days ago she was discharged from an outside hospital after an elective right hemicolectomy for complicated diverticulitis; during that admission she remained largely supine for the first four postoperative days, and since returning home she reports spending most hours in bed because of fatigue and abdominal discomfort, rising only for meals and toileting. She denies any history of malignancy, clotting disorder, or prior vein thrombosis, and she has not required a cast, brace, or splint on either lower extremity. Today she noted that her right ankle and lower calf appeared somewhat puffier than usual and that the skin over that region left an indentation when she pressed with her thumb, yet she did not observe comparable changes in the opposite limb. She specifically denies redness, warmth, or diffuse swelling from groin to foot, and she has not experienced sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, or palpitations. She denies recent trauma, fever, chills, weight loss, night sweats, or urinary complaints. Current medications include enteric-coated aspirin 81 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and postoperative oxycodone 5 mg taken sparingly for incisional pain; she is not on hormone replacement, oral contraceptives, or anticoagulation. Vital signs on arrival: temperature 98.6 °F, heart rate 86 bpm, blood pressure 138/78 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, SpO2 98% on room air. She appears tired but in no acute distress, speaking full sentences without dyspnea. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable with regular S1/S2, no murmurs, clear bilateral breath sounds, and no jugular venous distention. Abdominal surgical incision is clean, dry, and intact with mild perincisional tenderness expected for postoperative day 9, and no abdominal distention or guarding. Musculoskeletal and vascular examination of the legs reveals normal skin temperature bilaterally and symmetric palpable posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulses. The right lower calf and ankle demonstrate 1+ pitting edema that is not present on the left side; tape-measure circumference at 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity shows a difference of 1.2 cm. There is no cord-like induration, varicosities, or engorged superficial channels over either calf or thigh. Gentle palpation over the right femoral triangle, popliteal fossa, posterior calf, and along the course of the peroneal veins elicits no focal tenderness. Range of motion at the right knee and ankle is full and pain free except for the vague ache she describes deep in the posterior knee while standing. Sensation is intact and motor strength is 5/5 in all major muscle groups; there is no weakness, foot drop, or spasticity. Neurologic examination is otherwise nonfocal. Laboratory studies drawn in triage demonstrate hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelet count 235 × 10^3/µL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, INR 1.0; comprehensive metabolic panel is within normal limits. Given her recent major abdominal surgery less than two weeks prior, the prolonged time confined to bed greater than three consecutive days, and the new unilateral dependent edema, venous thromboembolic disease is high on the working differential. Cellulitis is considered less likely in the absence of erythema or warmth, and musculoskeletal strain does not fully account for the discrete pitting edema. No alternative explanation appears as compelling, and there is insufficient evidence to suggest a competing diagnosis of equal probability. Duplex ultrasonography of the right lower extremity has been ordered to further evaluate for deep venous occlusion, and anticoagulation decisions will be deferred pending imaging results and discussion with surgery regarding postoperative bleeding risk.

Explicitly stated 'elective right hemicolectomy nine days ago' (major surgery within 12 weeks) -> present. - Tenderness? Explicitly stated 'gentle palpation ... elicits no focal tenderness' -> absent. - Swelling? args:
A 67-year-old male with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C and hypertension was admitted after experiencing nausea, lightheadedness and a syncopal episode resulting in trauma to his head. En route to the hospital, the patient had one episode of hematemesis, followed by two additional episodes in the emergency department. Vital signs were as follows: heart rate 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure 131/71 mmHg and temperature 94.3°F. The patient was oriented to person, place and time. His abdomen was soft, non-distended and non-tender to palpation. Pertinent laboratory tests included hemoglobin 9.5 g/dl, hematocrit 29.5%, platelets 197 K/µL, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 22 mg/dl, creatinine 0.8 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 112 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 76 U/L, total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 18 U/L, albumin 3.2 g/dl, lactic acid 3.1 mmol/L, prothrombin time 11.8 seconds and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.0. Octreotide infusion, ceftriaxone and a proton-pump inhibitor were promptly started, along with transfusion of three units of blood due to persistent bleeding.
Urgent endoscopy was performed, where a significant amount of red blood was seen in the gastric fundus with an area of active bleeding near the gastroesophageal junction. No esophageal varices were seen. Attempts to control the hemorrhage endoscopically were unsuccessful due to large amount of active bleeding obscuring visibility. CT scan of the abdomen showed a 6-cm mass in the right lobe of a cirrhotic liver (suspected to be hepatocellular carcinoma [HCC]) and one large gastric varix with a gastrorenal shunt (Figure ). Importantly, there was no evidence of portal vein or splenic vein thrombosis.
Initially, left-sided TIPS was attempted, but multiple attempts to pass from left portal vein to left hepatic vein were unsuccessful due to challenging anatomy. The patient required multiple blood products. Eventually, the patient underwent BRTO. His left renal vein was accessed via the right internal jugular vein, the balloon was inflated and a sclerosing agent was injected into the bleeding varix. This was followed by coiling of the same varix, resulting in occlusion of the portosystemic shunt (Figure ). The patient had no further episodes of bleeding and was discharged a few days later. He was ultimately diagnosed with HCC (Figure ).

output.reasoning: Patient is 67-year-old male. The note mentions cirrhosis (implied by untreated hepatitis C history, cirrhotic liver on CT, and varices). Ascites not explicitly mentioned but implied by varices and liver mass; not tense. Encephalopathy: patient was oriented (absent). Albumin low at 3.2 g/dL (1 point). Bilirubin normal at 0.5 mg/dL (1 point). INR normal at 1.0 (1 point). No clear mention of ascites severity, but varices suggest chronic liver disease; assuming mild or absent. Prothrombin time normal. Child-Pugh criteria: Albumin 1pt, Bilirubin 1pt, INR 1pt, Encephalopathy 0pts, Ascites (absent/mild) 0pts. Total score 3.

A 67-year-old male with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C and hypertension was admitted after experiencing nausea, lightheadedness and a syncopal episode resulting in trauma to his head. En route to the hospital, the patient had one episode of hematemesis, followed by two additional episodes in the emergency department. Vital signs were as follows: heart rate 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure 131/71 mmHg and temperature 94.3°F. The patient was oriented to person, place and time. His abdomen was soft, non-distended and non-tender to palpation. Pertinent laboratory tests included hemoglobin 9.5 g/dl, hematocrit 29.5%, platelets 197 K/µL, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 22 mg/dl, creatinine 0.8 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 112 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 76 U/L, total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 18 U/L, albumin 3.2 g/dl, lactic acid 3.1 mmol/L, prothrombin time 11.8 seconds and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.0. Octreotide infusion, ceftriaxone and a proton-pump inhibitor were promptly started, along with transfusion of three units of blood due to persistent bleeding.

args:
A 67-year-old male with a past medical history of untreated hepatitis C and hypertension was admitted after experiencing nausea, lightheadedness and a syncopal episode resulting in trauma to his head. En route to the hospital, the patient had one episode of hematemesis, followed by two additional episodes in the emergency department. Vital signs were as follows: heart rate 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, blood pressure 131/71 mmHg and temperature 94.3°F. The patient was oriented to person, place and time. His abdomen was soft, non-distended and non-tender to palpation. Pertinent laboratory tests included hemoglobin 9.5 g/dl, hematocrit 29.5%, platelets 197 K/µL, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 22 mg/dl, creatinine 0.8 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 112 U/L, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 76 U/L, total bilirubin 0.5 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 18 U/L, albumin 3.2 g/dl, lactic acid 3.1 mmol/L, prothrombin time 11.8 seconds and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.0. Octreotide infusion, ceftriaxone and a proton-pump inhibitor were promptly started, along with transfusion of three units of blood due to persistent bleeding.
Urgent endoscopy was performed, where a significant amount of red blood was seen in the gastric fundus with an area of active bleeding near the gastroesophageal junction. No esophageal varices were seen. Attempts to control the hemorrhage endoscopically were unsuccessful due to large amount of active bleeding obscuring visibility. CT scan of the abdomen showed a 6-cm mass in the right lobe of a cirrhotic liver (suspected to be hepatocellular carcinoma [HCC]) and one large gastric varix with a gastrorenal shunt (Figure ). Importantly, there was no evidence of portal vein or splenic vein thrombosis.
Initially, left-sided TIPS was attempted, but multiple attempts to pass from left portal vein to left hepatic vein were unsuccessful due to challenging anatomy. The patient required multiple blood products. Eventually, the patient underwent BRTO. His left renal vein was accessed via the right internal jugular vein, the balloon was inflated and a sclerosing agent was injected into the bleeding varix. This was followed by coiling of the same varix, resulting in occlusion of the portosystemic shunt (Figure ). The patient had no further episodes of bleeding and was discharged a few days later. He was ultimately diagnosed with HCC (Figure ).

def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> float:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    Return the final numeric answer.
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

```

A 70-year-old man with a past medical history significant for hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage 4, and prostate cancer stage 4 on outpatient chemotherapy at another facility presented to our emergency department with complaints of vomiting and nausea a day prior to admission. He had three episodes of nonbloody, non-bilious, and nonbloody diarrhea as well. The patient had one-day history of productive cough with whitish sputum and subjective fevers. Patient, though adherent with chemotherapy, had admitted that he was not able to take his other home medications. In the ED, patient had a temperature of 103.1degrees F, heart rate of 98 beats per minute, blood pressure of 102/55 mmHg, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, glucose of 121 mg/dl. Physical exam was significant for conscious, alert and oriented to person, place, and time; pink and moist mucosa, right surgical scar at the right hypochondriac, soft, nontender, bowel sounds were present. Pertinent abnormal labs include: white blood cell count (WBC), 14.5 units/mcl (reference range: 4.80-10.80 units/mcl) with neutrophils of 82.1% (reference range: 44%-70%), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 39 mg/dl (reference range: 7-18 mg/dl), creatinine (Cr) 3.8 mg/dl (reference range: 0.7-1.2 mg/dl), calcium (Ca) 7.7 mg/dl (reference range: 8.5-10.1 mg/dl). Chest x-ray showed a focal area of consolidation involving the left midlung. Patient was admitted and managed on the medical floor for sepsis likely secondary hospital acquired pneumonia especially given his immunocompromised state. Patient was started on IV vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam in the emergency department.
During the course of the hospitalization, infectious disease was consulted and they recommended to start the patient on IV cefepime 1 g Q24 hours, oral linezolid 600 mg Q12H, and oral clindamycin 450 mg Q8H. In addition, patient was started on calcium acetate for his hypocalcemia and was continued on sodium bicarbonate 1300 mg Q8H for his AKI on CKD. After two days the cefepime dose was increased to 2 mg. Two days after increasing the dose of cefepime, patient started developing altered mental status. Patient was alert and oriented only to person, incoherent and expressed thoughts like “I would kill myself if I had the chance” so the cefepime was discontinued and within one day the patient was more conversant, denied any suicidal ideations and was alert and oriented to person and place. In addition, the BUN and creatinine (Cr) levels trended up to 41 mg/dl and 5.4 mg/dl. So the calcium acetate was switched to calcium carbonate and calcitriol. After making these changes the Cr level trended down to 3.4 mg/dl. In addition, the corrected Calcium level was at normal levels based on the albumin level of the patient. The mental status improved and patient was subsequently discharged. Table provides the electrolyte levels.

Urea > 7 mmol/L - present (BUN 39 mg/dl ≈ 13.9 mmol/L, >7 mmol/L). Respiratory rate ≥30/min - absent (RR 18/min). args:
A 70-year-old man with a past medical history significant for hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage 4, and prostate cancer stage 4 on outpatient chemotherapy at another facility presented to our emergency department with complaints of vomiting and nausea a day prior to admission. He had three episodes of nonbloody, non-bilious, and nonbloody diarrhea as well. The patient had one-day history of productive cough with whitish sputum and subjective fevers. Patient, though adherent with chemotherapy, had admitted that he was not able to take his other home medications. In the ED, patient had a temperature of 103.1degrees F, heart rate of 98 beats per minute, blood pressure of 102/55 mmHg, respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, glucose of 121 mg/dl. Physical exam was significant for conscious, alert and oriented to person, place, and time; pink and moist mucosa, right surgical scar at the right hypochondriac, soft, nontender, bowel sounds were present. Pertinent abnormal labs include: white blood cell count (WBC), 14.5 units/mcl (reference range: 4.80-10.80 units/mcl) with neutrophils of 82.1% (reference range: 44%-70%), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 39 mg/dl (reference range: 7-18 mg/dl), creatinine (Cr) 3.8 mg/dl (reference range: 0.7-1.2 mg/dl), calcium (Ca) 7.7 mg/dl (reference range: 8.5-10.1 mg/dl). Chest x-ray showed a focal area of consolidation involving the left midlung. Patient was admitted and managed on the medical floor for sepsis likely secondary hospital acquired pneumonia especially given his immunocompromised state. Patient was started on IV vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam in the emergency department.
During the course of the hospitalization, infectious disease was consulted and they recommended to start the patient on IV cefepime 1 g Q24 hours, oral linezolid 600 mg Q12H, and oral clindamycin 450 mg Q8H. In addition, patient was started on calcium acetate for his hypocalcemia and was continued on sodium bicarbonate 1300 mg Q8H for his AKI on CKD. After two days the cefepime dose was increased to 2 mg. Two days after increasing the dose of cefepime, patient started developing altered mental status. Patient was alert and oriented only to person, incoherent and expressed thoughts like “I would kill myself if I had the chance” so the cefepime was discontinued and within one day the patient was more conversant, denied any suicidal ideations and was alert and oriented to person and place. In addition, the BUN and creatinine (Cr) levels trended up to 41 mg/dl and 5.4 mg/dl. So the calcium acetate was switched to calcium carbonate and calcitriol. After making these changes the Cr level trended down to 3.4 mg/dl. In addition, the corrected Calcium level was at normal levels based on the albumin level of the patient. The mental status improved and patient was subsequently discharged. Table provides the electrolyte levels.

confusion: New-onset confusion (True/False)
bun_elevated: BUN > 19 mg/dL or urea > 7 mmol/L (True/False)
respiratory_rate_elevated: Respiratory rate >= 30 (True/False)
hypotension: SBP < 90 or DBP <= 60 mmHg (True/False)
age_65_plus: Age >= 65 years (True/False)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: confusion, elevated_urea, low_blood_pressure, age_65_or_older
- Conditions ABSENT: respiratory_rate_30_or_more
- Demographics: Age=70, Sex=male

args:
A 37-year-old African American female patient morbidly obese with no other significant medical history, presented to the ED complaining of chest pain. She described her chest pain as pressure like and tightness in the middle of chest, 7 out of 10 in severity, lasting for five to six minutes then gradually improving and lingering on for some more minutes before completely wearing off. Pain radiated to her back and was not related to activity. The patient denied shortness of breath, nausea, and vomiting. She denied history of heart disease, previous similar chest pain or substance abuse, and she stated that she is quite healthy otherwise.
Physical exam revealed stable vital signs -- blood pressure: 123/78 mmHg, heart rate: 73 beats/min, respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min, temperature: 36.5 degrees Celsius, SpO2: 97% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a pulse with regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, with no murmurs or added sounds. Respiratory examination was normal with normal vesicular breath sounds heard bilaterally. The rest of physical examination was unremarkable. Urine toxicology and pregnancy test were negative, other laboratory tests were within normal limits.
The EKG showed normal sinus rhythm with no ischemic changes, serial high sensitivity troponin was elevated at 440>606>872 ng/mL (normal range 0-0.4 ng/mL). Due to concern of aortic dissection and PE, computed tomography angiography (CTA) and CT PE were performed and both were negative. Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed normal left ventricular ejection fraction and no regional wall motion abnormalities. Due to her significant chest pain and up trending of troponin, the patient had a LHC. LHC showed 70% stenosis of the first obtuse with type A dissection (Figure ).
Intravascular ultrasound (IVUS) showed type A spontaneous coronary dissection with no significant compromise in lumen as shown in Figure . The patient was monitored in the cardiac telemetry unit for one day, she was medically stable, and her chest pain resolved. The patient was discharged on aspirin, metoprolol, and atorvastatin.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 37-year-old female. HEART Score criteria: History (chest pain described as "pressure like and tightness", 7/10 severity, radiating to back, not activity-related - moderately suspicious). EKG shows normal sinus rhythm with no ischemic changes (normal). Age is 37 (<45 = 0 points). Risk factors: morbid obesity present, but no history of heart disease, diabetes, hypertension, smoking, or family history mentioned. Troponin elevated (440>606>872 ng/mL) which is significantly above normal (0-0.4 ng/mL), indicating positive troponin. No other risk factors identified. Conditions present: chest_pain_moderate_suspicion, troponin_positive. Conditions absent: chest_pain_high_suspicion, ekg_ischemic, age_45_older, risk_factors_3_or_more.

The patient was monitored in the cardiac telemetry unit for one day, she was medically stable, and her chest pain resolved. The patient was discharged on aspirin, metoprolol, and atorvastatin.

calculator_name: CHA2DS2-VASc Score for Atrial Fibrillation Stroke Risk

args: What is the patient's CHA2DS2-VASc Score? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

args:
Our patient is a 38-year-old woman with a past medical history most significant for a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery in March 2017, complicated by a marginal ulcer at the anastomosis site with subsequent perforation. The patient could not tolerate oral nutrition and became severely malnourished, and a PICC line was placed for TPN in December 2017. Chronic TPN use led to recurrent candidemia with blood cultures growing Candida albicans resistant to fluconazole and voriconazole. Over several months, her PICC line was replaced thrice, and she was ultimately treated with micafungin. The patient had multiple admissions over the past few months for workup of altered mental status and unsteady gait. Her other past medical history included systemic lupus erythematosus treated with mycophenolate mofetil and hydroxychloroquine, left portal vein branch thrombosis on enoxaparin therapy, type 2 diabetes mellitus not on insulin, fibromyalgia, depression, and anxiety. She presented to our hospital in July 2018 with complaints of tremors, impaired mobility, confusion, gait instability, falls, and lethargy.
On admission, she was noted to be confused, with horizontal nystagmus and dilated pupils as well as bradypnea. Dilated fundus examination was indicative of candida retinitis. She was lethargic but arousable. Her speech was difficult to understand, and the history of presenting illness was provided primarily by the patient's mother who relayed to the team that she had been having tremors, episodes of confusion, and numerous falls for the past several months. Her mother also noticed that she had increased irritability and emotional lability during this time. She was initially admitted to the intensive care unit for airway management due to her acute encephalopathy. Due to a lack of meningeal signs, a lumbar puncture was not performed.
The infectious disease service was consulted, and she was started on liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine for a total of 6 weeks. MRI brain showed worsening T1 hyperintensities from June 2018 to July 2018 in the bilateral globus pallidi, as evidenced by the arrows in Figures and , which were thought to be secondary to manganese toxicity from chronic TPN use. Furthermore, a repeat MRI brain was performed in August 2018, one month after discontinuation of TPN, which showed improvement of the T1 hyperintensities in the bilateral globus pallidi, as evidenced by the white arrows in , approaching the intensity level noted in June 2018. Other supporting findings included T1 hyperintensities in the substantia nigra, as evidenced by the arrows in , and decreased signal in the bilateral globus pallidi on T2-weighted images, as evidenced by the arrows in . With antifungal treatment and cessation of TPN, her mentation and neurological symptoms improved within 3-4 days of cessation of TPN.
General surgery was consulted for gastrostomy tube placement in her remnant stomach, as the patient was unable to consume more than 500 calories by mouth per day due to complaints of nausea and vomiting. Although our patient presented to the hospital seven months after initiation of TPN therapy, her Parkinsonian symptoms had begun to manifest after three months on TPN.

output.reasoning: Patient is 38 years old (<65=0pts). Female. No explicit or implied atrial fibrillation (AF) mentioned. No history of heart failure (CHF) or reduced ejection fraction. No stroke/TIA/thromboembolism history. No vascular disease (MI, PAD, aortic plaque) mentioned. Hypertension not mentioned or implied (no antihypertensive medications listed). Diabetes mellitus type 2 present (1pt). Demographics: age 38, sex female.

def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> float:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    Return the final numeric answer.
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

```

args: A 40-year-old man was referred to gastrointestinal surgeons for pancreatic mass pointed out by an abdominal ultrasonography in a medical check-up. The patient did not show any symptoms. Abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) revealed dissection from the CA (), DPA, and splenic artery (SA). This aneurysm was not seen on an abdominal unenhanced CT taken 6 years ago in a medical check-up. The enhancing inner lumen of the DPA was derived from false lumen and that of the SA was derived from true lumen. The stenosis of proximal side of SA, an aneurysm measuring 2.5 cm on DPAA with unenhanced mural thrombus, and the connection of the distal side of the DPAA to transverse pancreatic artery (TPA) with stenotic change were confirmed (). A connection with DPA and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) was undetermined. The current diagnosis was the dissection of the CA to DPA and SA, resulting in DPAA and SA stenosis. After the diagnosis was made, the patient was referred to the department of radiology by a vascular surgeon for the consultation of endovascular surgery to prevent aneurysm rupture because open surgery was thought to be quite invasive as it required distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy and CA repair. He had received medications for hypertension. There were no remarkable findings in his initial laboratory data. We planned to embolize DPAA, SA, and dissected area of CA that allow to preserve vascular supply to hepatic artery from SMA via peripancreatic arcade and to distal side of SA via gastroduodenal artery (GDA) to TPA. An angiography from the CA revealed the enhancing inner lumen of DPAA from false lumen of dissected CA connecting to TPA (). TPA was revealed between the peripancreatic arcade from GDA and pancreatic tail artery that connected to the distal side of SA. A 4.5 Fr. Shepherd hook–type guiding sheath (Parent Plus 45: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), carrying a triple coaxial system, consisted of a 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (Cerulean G: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), 2.6 Fr. high-flow catheter (Masters HF: Asahi Intech, Aichi, Japan), 1.9 Fr. microcatheter (Carnelian Marvel: Tokai Medical Products, Aichi, Japan), was hooked on the CA. To prevent the coil migration into the TPA, a microballoon catheter (Logos: Piolax, Yokohama, Japan) was advanced to the distal side of the DPAA via the GDA to pancreatoduodenal artery and inflated at the sufficient point of the TPA through the additional 4 Fr. shepherd hook catheter placed in the CA from the contralateral groin site (). The 4.5 Fr guiding sheath and the 4 Fr. Shepherd hook catheter were inserted from the right and left side of the common femoral artery, respectively. Under microballoon catheter inflation, the distal side of the DPAA was embolized with detachable coils (PRESIDIO 6 mm × 20 cm, GALAXY 6 mm × 15 cm) through a 1.9 Fr microcatheter (). Afterward, the microballoon catheter was deflated. Then, intra-aneurysmal coil embolization was performed with detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 20 cm) through the 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (). Later, the microcatheter in the intermediate catheter was carefully advanced through the dissected portion of the SA and embolization of the affected area was performed with detachable coils (Penumbra POD 6 mm × 50 cm, Penumbra 6 mm × 15 cm, 4 mm × 8 cm, GALAXY 2 of 7 mm × 21 cm, 2 of 4 mm × 15 cm) (). Finally, coil embolization of the dissected CA was performed using detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, 4 mm × 10 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, Penumbra coil 400 2 of 10 mm × 30 cm) (). The final angiography from SMA after pulling out all the systems revealed complete occlusion of the DPAA, the proximal side of SA, preserving GDA to the TPA to the distal side of SA and preserving GDA to common hepatic artery to left gastric artery (). No complication including post-coiling ischemia or transient laboratory abnormalities in transaminases, amylases, or lipase was observed during and after the endovascular surgery. The patient was followed up for 2 years at the near hospital; however, DPAA has not shown regrowth by contrast enhancement CT.

output.reasoning: Patient is a 40-year-old man (demographics). Hypertension is explicitly mentioned as being treated with medications. No other comorbidities (myocardial infarction, congestive heart failure, peripheral vascular disease, cerebrovascular disease, dementia, chronic pulmonary disease, rheumatic disease, peptic ulcer disease, liver disease, diabetes, hemiplegia, renal disease, cancer, metastatic disease, AIDS) are mentioned or implied. Pancreatic mass was found but described as asymptomatic and not confirmed as malignant; no cancer treatment mentioned. No evidence of other Charlson conditions.

args: A 40-year-old man was referred to gastrointestinal surgeons for pancreatic mass pointed out by an abdominal ultrasonography in a medical check-up. The patient did not show any symptoms. Abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) revealed dissection from the CA (), DPA, and splenic artery (SA). This aneurysm was not seen on an abdominal unenhanced CT taken 6 years ago in a medical check-up. The enhancing inner lumen of the DPA was derived from false lumen and that of the SA was derived from true lumen. The stenosis of proximal side of SA, an aneurysm measuring 2.5 cm on DPAA with unenhanced mural thrombus, and the connection of the distal side of the DPAA to transverse pancreatic artery (TPA) with stenotic change were confirmed (). A connection with DPA and the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) was undetermined. The current diagnosis was the dissection of the CA to DPA and SA, resulting in DPAA and SA stenosis. After the diagnosis was made, the patient was referred to the department of radiology by a vascular surgeon for the consultation of endovascular surgery to prevent aneurysm rupture because open surgery was thought to be quite invasive as it required distal pancreatectomy with splenectomy and CA repair. He had received medications for hypertension. There were no remarkable findings in his initial laboratory data. We planned to embolize DPAA, SA, and dissected area of CA that allow to preserve vascular supply to hepatic artery from SMA via peripancreatic arcade and to distal side of SA via gastroduodenal artery (GDA) to TPA. An angiography from the CA revealed the enhancing inner lumen of DPAA from false lumen of dissected CA connecting to TPA (). TPA was revealed between the peripancreatic arcade from GDA and pancreatic tail artery that connected to the distal side of SA. A 4.5 Fr. Shepherd hook–type guiding sheath (Parent Plus 45: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), carrying a triple coaxial system, consisted of a 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (Cerulean G: Medikit, Tokyo, Japan), 2.6 Fr. high-flow catheter (Masters HF: Asahi Intech, Aichi, Japan), 1.9 Fr. microcatheter (Carnelian Marvel: Tokai Medical Products, Aichi, Japan), was hooked on the CA. To prevent the coil migration into the TPA, a microballoon catheter (Logos: Piolax, Yokohama, Japan) was advanced to the distal side of the DPAA via the GDA to pancreatoduodenal artery and inflated at the sufficient point of the TPA through the additional 4 Fr. shepherd hook catheter placed in the CA from the contralateral groin site (). The 4.5 Fr guiding sheath and the 4 Fr. Shepherd hook catheter were inserted from the right and left side of the common femoral artery, respectively. Under microballoon catheter inflation, the distal side of the DPAA was embolized with detachable coils (PRESIDIO 6 mm × 20 cm, GALAXY 6 mm × 15 cm) through a 1.9 Fr microcatheter (). Afterward, the microballoon catheter was deflated. Then, intra-aneurysmal coil embolization was performed with detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 20 cm) through the 4 Fr. intermediate catheter (). Later, the microcatheter in the intermediate catheter was carefully advanced through the dissected portion of the SA and embolization of the affected area was performed with detachable coils (Penumbra POD 6 mm × 50 cm, Penumbra 6 mm × 15 cm, 4 mm × 8 cm, GALAXY 2 of 7 mm × 21 cm, 2 of 4 mm × 15 cm) (). Finally, coil embolization of the dissected CA was performed using detachable coils (Interlock-35 10 mm × 40 cm, 2 of 8 mm × 40 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, 4 mm × 10 cm, 6 mm × 10 cm, Penumbra coil 400 2 of 10 mm × 30 cm) (). The final angiography from SMA after pulling out all the systems revealed complete occlusion of the DPAA, the proximal side of SA, preserving GDA to the TPA to the distal side of SA and preserving GDA to common hepatic artery to left gastric artery (). No complication including post-coiling ischemia or transient laboratory abnormalities in transaminases, amylases, or lipase was observed during and after the endovascular surgery. The patient was followed up for 2 years at the near hospital; however, DPAA has not shown regrowth by contrast enhancement CT.

age: Patient age (1 pt per decade over 40, max 4)
mi: Myocardial infarction (+1)
chf: Congestive heart failure (+1)
pvd: Peripheral vascular disease (+1)
cva: Cerebrovascular disease/TIA (+1)
dementia: Dementia (+1)
copd: COPD (+1)
connective_tissue: Connective tissue disease (+1)
pud: Peptic ulcer disease (+1)
mild_liver: Mild liver disease (+1)
diabetes: Diabetes without complications (+1)
hemiplegia: Hemiplegia (+2)
moderate_renal: Moderate/severe renal disease (+2)
diabetes_complications: Diabetes with complications (+2)
malignancy: Any malignancy (+2)
moderate_liver: Moderate/severe liver disease (+3)

aids: AIDS (+6)

- Demographics: Age=40, Sex=male

```python
def simulate_medical_value(patient_note: str, question: str) -> float:
    """Calculate a medical value from a patient note.

    Calculate a medical value from a patient note.
    
    Given a patient note and a calculation question, reason step by step:
    1. Carefully read the patient note to extract all relevant clinical values
    2. Identify what medical calculation/score is needed
    3. Apply the appropriate formula from the reference below
    4. Show your calculation step by step, checking your arithmetic
    
    FORMULAS (use these exact formulas):
    - APACHE II Score: Acute Physiology Score + Age Points + Chronic Health Points
    - Adjusted Body Weight: ABW = IBW + 0.4 * (actual_weight - IBW)
    - Albumin Corrected Anion Gap: Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 * (4 - albumin)
    - Anion Gap: AG = Na - (Cl + HCO3)
    - Body Mass Index (BMI): BMI = weight_kg / (height_m)^2
    - Body Surface Area (Mosteller): BSA = sqrt((height_cm * weight_kg) / 3600)
    - CHA2DS2-VASc Score: Sum of: CHF(+1), HTN(+1), Age>=75(+2)/65-74(+1), DM(+1), Stroke/TIA(+2), Vascular(+1), Female(+1)
    - CKD-EPI GFR (2021): 142 * min(Cr/kappa,1)^alpha * max(Cr/kappa,1)^-1.200 * 0.9938^age * sex_coef
    - CURB-65 Score: C + U + R + B + 65 (0-5 points)
    - Calcium Correction for Hypoalbuminemia: Corrected Ca = Ca + 0.8 * (4 - albumin)
    - Caprini VTE Score: Sum of weighted risk factors
    - Centor Score (McIsaac): Sum with age modifier
    - Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI): Sum of weighted comorbidity points + age points
    - Child-Pugh Score: Sum of points for bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy
    - Creatinine Clearance (Cockcroft-Gault): CrCl = ((140 - age) * adjusted_weight * gender_coef) / (Cr * 72)
    - Date of Conception: gestational_weeks - 2 (conception ~2 weeks after LMP)
    - Delta Gap: Delta Gap = AG - 12 (where 12 is normal AG)
    - Delta Ratio: Delta Ratio = Delta Gap / (24 - HCO3)
    - Estimated Due Date: 40 - current_gestational_age
    - FIB-4 Index: FIB-4 = (Age * AST) / (Platelets * sqrt(ALT))
    - FeverPAIN Score: Fever + Purulence + Attend rapidly + Inflamed tonsils + No cough/coryza (0-5)
    - Fractional Excretion of Sodium (FENa): FENa = (UNa * SCr) / (SNa * UCr) * 100
    - Framingham Risk Score: Complex multivariate equation based on age, sex, lipids, BP, smoking
    - Free Water Deficit: FWD = TBW% * weight * (Na/140 - 1)
    - Gestational Age: weeks + days/7
    - Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): Eye + Verbal + Motor (3-15)
    - Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS): Sum of clinical and lab criteria (0-23)
    - HAS-BLED Score: H-A-S-B-L-E-D criteria (0-9 points)
    - HEART Score: Sum of History + ECG + Age + Risk factors + Troponin (each 0-2 points)
    - HOMA-IR: HOMA-IR = (Glucose * Insulin) / 405
    - Ideal Body Weight (Devine): Male: IBW = 50 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60), Female: IBW = 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
    - LDL Calculated (Friedewald): LDL = Total Cholesterol - HDL - (Triglycerides / 5)
    - MDRD GFR: 175 * (Cr)^-1.154 * (age)^-0.203 * sex_coef
    - MELD-Na Score: MELD = 10 * (0.957*ln(Cr) + 0.378*ln(Bili) + 1.120*ln(INR) + 0.643)
    - Maintenance Fluids (4-2-1 Rule): First 10kg: 4ml/kg/hr, Next 10kg: 2ml/kg/hr, Remaining: 1ml/kg/hr
    - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): MAP = (2 * DBP + SBP) / 3
    - Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME): Sum of (opioid dose * conversion factor)
    - PERC Rule: 8 criteria; PERC negative if all 0
    - PSI/PORT Score: Demographics + Comorbidities + Physical Exam + Labs
    - QTc (Bazett): QTc = QT / sqrt(RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Framingham): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Fridericia): QTc = QT / RR^(1/3) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - QTc (Hodges): QTc = QT + 1.75 * (HR - 60)
    - QTc (Rautaharju): QTc = QT + 154 * (1 - RR) where RR = 60/HR in seconds
    - Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI): Sum of 6 risk factors (0-6 points)
    - SIRS Criteria: Count of criteria met (>= 2 = SIRS positive)
    - SOFA Score: Sum of 6 organ system scores (0-24)
    - Serum Osmolality: Osm = 2*Na + BUN/2.8 + Glucose/18
    - Sodium Correction for Hyperglycemia: Corrected Na = Na + 0.024 * (glucose - 100)
    - Steroid Conversion: Prednisone equivalent = dose * conversion_factor
    - Target Weight: Target Weight = target_bmi * (height_m)^2
    - Wells' Criteria for DVT: Sum of clinical criteria points
    - Wells' Criteria for PE: Sum of weighted criteria
    
    Important:
    - Be precise with extracted values. Double-check your arithmetic.
    - For sex/gender: "man"/"male"/"he" → male, "woman"/"female"/"she" → female.
    - Convert units as needed (lbs→kg: ×0.453592, feet/inches→cm: (ft×12+in)×2.54).
    - For scoring systems: carefully check each criterion against the patient note.
      Look for conditions implied by medications or clinical findings, not just
      explicitly named conditions.
    
    Return the final numeric answer.
    
    Examples:
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 70-year-old male weighing 80 kg, height 175 cm.", "What is the patient's BMI?")
    26.122
    >>> calculate_medical_value("A 65-year-old female, BP 130/85 mmHg.", "What is the patient's Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?")
    100.0
    """
    ...

```

args: An 80-year-old male patient with a 4-year history of chronic NV-AF, type 2 diabetes mellitus and a history of dyslipidaemia presented to the emergency department with general weakness, nausea, vomiting of blood-tinged material, vague abdominal pain and black stool of 1 day’s duration. He denied fever or diarrhoea. His regular medications included dabigatran 110 mg twice daily for 4 years to prevent thromboembolic events, metformin 1,000 mg twice daily and insulin glargine 20 units at night. The last dose of dabigatran was on the morning of presentation. There were no previous episodes of bleeding or decreases in haemoglobin since dabigatran was started 4 years previously. No new medications had been administered recently. The patient denied alcohol or tobacco consumption. However, he mentioned the recent use, without physician counselling, of 200 ml of a boiled mixture of ginger and cinnamon twice daily for 3 days before presentation to hospital. Physical examination was remarkable for overweight (body mass index 28 kg/m2), skin pallor, irregular tachycardia (115–125 bpm), tachypnoea (respiratory rate of 28/minute), low blood pressure (80/60 mmHg) and dry oral mucosa. His oxygen saturation was 94% on room air. Cardiovascular examination was remarkable for irregular tachycardia, chest examination was normal, and abdominal examination revealed mild tenderness all over the abdomen. His extremities were cold. Digital rectal examination showed melena. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable. On presentation, the patient had a haemoglobin (Hb) of 8 g/dl (normal 13.5–17 g/dl), haematocrit (Hct) of 24% (normal 45–52%) microcytic hypochromic anaemia, reticulocyte index >2, platelet count of 600,000 (normal range 150,000–450,000/l), International Normalized Ratio (INR) of 1.9 (normal INR 1.1 or less), and activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) of 45 sec (normal 30–40 sec). His blood glucose level was 142 mg/dl (normal 70–140 mg/dl) and kidney function was normal (eGFR 90 ml/min/1.73 m2, estimated by CKD-EPI). Serum sodium was 140 mEq/l, serum potassium was 4.5 mEq/l, and a liver function test was normal. As the history and clinical picture were suggestive of gastrointestinal bleeding with an element of haemorrhagic shock, resuscitation measures were started in the emergency department with administration of intravenous fluid and the patient was moved to the intensive care unit. He later developed severe haematemesis after which the trachea was intubated and the patient was mechanically ventilated. Packed red blood cell transfusion was started. As the patient had taken his last dose of dabigatran 110 mg on the morning of admission, idarucizumab, a reversal agent of dabigatran, was administered (5 g intravenously according to protocol). Urgent upper endoscopy was performed and showed diffuse haemorrhage of the mucosal membrane with fresh blood and blood clots in the oesophagus, stomach and duodenum; no definite ulcers or oesophageal varices were identified. The resuscitation measures were continued with intravenous fluid (crystalloid and colloid) and blood products, but bleeding persisted with fresh blood from the nasogastric tube. Haemorrhagic shock continued despite volume replacement (fluid and blood) and vasopressor support, and the patient died within 24 hours. The history of recent combination of herbal products (ginger and cinnamon) with DOACs (dabigatran) and the presence of diffuse haemorrhage of the mucosal membrane of the upper gastrointestinal tract raised the possibility of a herb–drug interaction leading to severe gastrointestinal bleeding.

args: A 57-year-old female with a background of mild chronic joint aches and no known advanced comorbidities presented to the emergency department complaining of progressive malaise, persistent dizziness, and intermittent fever for the past three days. She noted a loss of appetite during this period, along with a decline in her usual level of activity. Upon arrival, she was found to be breathing on her own but requiring moderate supplementation of inspired air to maintain adequate oxygenation, and her arterial blood sample showed a partial pressure of oxygen measuring 86 mm Hg with an FiO2 of 35% oxygen. Clinicians noted that she appeared lethargic, with slurred speech and disorientation to time and place; a formal neurological assessment gave her a score of 9 for GCS. Concern also arose from the fact that she had a blood pressure recorded at 65/58 mm Hg, deemed low enough to prompt immediate investigation into her circulatory status. She was receiving dopamine  3 mcg/kg/min to augment cardiac performance and norepinephrine 0.04 mcg/kg/min to maintain vascular tone. Laboratory data performed shortly after her arrival highlighted several key abnormalities. Platelet count was measured at 140,000 per microliter, falling below normal limits but not at a level typically associated with severe bleeding risk. Her total bilirubin, meanwhile, was reported as 1.9 mg/dL, slightly elevated and suggestive of either hepatic involvement or hemolysis, though it was not high enough to cause overt jaundice or other acute liver failure indicators. With regard to renal status, her serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL placed her at the higher end of normal for her age, and her reported urine output of 1000 milliliters per day raised questions about whether she was receiving adequate fluid intake or whether early renal hypoperfusion might be playing a role in limiting her urine production. She denied any prolonged use of nephrotoxic agents, and her caregiver mentioned that she had been able to produce urine regularly at home until her energy levels fell. She displayed no signs of airway compromise that warranted mechanical support at the time she was first seen, which allowed the team to continue monitoring her spontaneous ventilation closely. Despite her moderate confusion, she was intermittently able to follow simple commands, and her speech—though mumbled—was at times coherent. Her peripheral perfusion exam revealed cool extremities, which correlated with her somewhat low blood pressure readings, but no overt signs of mottling were present. Auscultation of her chest was largely unremarkable except for a few scattered crackles at the bases, and her heart sounds were distant but otherwise normal in rhythm. She was afebrile on initial measurement in the department, but her relative tachycardia and report of fevers at home kept the clinicians vigilant for possible infection. Given her state, screening tests for sepsis and other inflammatory conditions were rapidly initiated, and a broad inquiry into her recent exposures, including dietary habits and any recent travel, was conducted. Family members indicated that she had not taken any newly prescribed medications and had no recorded history of severe allergic reactions, although she did acknowledge having had mild arthritic pain for several years. The plan at the time of presentation, before any escalated interventions, focused on stabilizing her circulation, maintaining her respiratory status through continued oxygen supplementation, and initiating a thorough diagnostic workup, including imaging of the chest and abdomen as needed, to locate any source of infection or inflammation. Confirmatory testing for possible hepatic or biliary issues was also ordered in light of her bilirubin result, and careful measurement of her urinary output was set to continue to gauge her renal perfusion and response to any fluid adjustments. The team remained alert to the possibility that she could deteriorate without prompt treatment, so close observation was prioritized. Despite her drowsiness and the borderline platelet count, no heavy sedation or immediate intubation was undertaken at the point she arrived, as the clinical judgment leaned toward aggressive monitoring with appropriate supportive measures while investigating the underlying cause of her presentation, given her partially preserved respiratory drive and ability to respond on a limited basis.

output.reasoning: Analyzed patient note for SOFA Score criteria. Demographics: female, age 57. Respiratory: PaO2 86 mm Hg on FiO2 35% (0.35) -> PaO2/FiO2 ratio = 86/0.35 = 245.7 (moderate respiratory failure, 2 points). Coagulation: platelets 140,000/μL (mild thrombocytopenia, 1 point). Liver: bilirubin 1.9 mg/dL (mild elevation, 1 point). Cardiovascular: hypotension (BP 65/58) requiring dopamine and norepinephrine (vasopressors, 3 points). CNS: GCS 9 (moderate impairment, 2 points). Renal: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (normal, 0 points) and urine output 1000 mL/day (adequate, 0 points). No explicit mention of chronic organ failures or other SOFA components. Conditions present: respiratory_failure, thrombocytopenia, hyperbilirubinemia, hypotension_requiring_vasopressors, neurological_impairment. Conditions absent: severe_thrombocytopenia, severe_liver_failure, renal_failure.
